title: SEGMENT 4 — The alpha-gal knockout pig: the first crack in the wall

lines.text: SEGMENT 4 — The alpha-gal knockout pig: the first crack in the wall
The year is 2002.
CRISPR doesn't exist yet — it won't be invented as a tool for another decade.
But people had developed an older, slower, much more expensive technique for making genetically engineered pigs.
The technique combined two earlier breakthroughs.
The first was somatic cell nuclear transfer — the cloning technology that produced Dolly the sheep in 1996.
The second was a method for knocking out genes in cells in culture using something called homologous recombination — a slow, low-efficiency, but functional way of disabling a specific gene.
The recipe went like this.
Take pig fibroblasts — skin cells — and grow them in a dish.
Disable the GalT gene in those cells using homologous recombination.
Screen for the rare cells where the knockout worked, which might be one in a million.
Once you find such a cell, take its nucleus, inject it into a pig egg whose own nucleus has been removed.
Implant the resulting reconstructed egg into a surrogate sow.
Wait three months and three weeks and three days.
Out comes a piglet whose every cell carries the GalT knockout — because every cell traces back to that one engineered fibroblast.
This was, at the time, an extraordinarily difficult procedure.
Each step was inefficient.
Most attempts failed.
The animals that did get born often had developmental defects from the cloning.

But in 2002, a company called Immerge BioTherapeutics, and shortly afterward a company called PPL Therapeutics (the same outfit that had cloned Dolly), published papers announcing successful GalT knockout pigs.
The first GalT-KO pig — sometimes called the alpha-gal-free pig, or the GTKO pig — was born.
These pigs are normal pigs.
They walk around, they eat, they grow, they have piglets.
The GalT enzyme isn't essential for pig life — they get by without alpha-gal on their cells just fine.
But their organs, when looked at under a microscope and stained for alpha-gal, are clean.
The sugar is gone.

And when you transplant a heart or a kidney from a GalT-KO pig into a baboon — which is the standard preclinical xenotransplant model, because baboons, like humans, lack alpha-gal and make anti-alpha-gal antibodies — the result is dramatically different from the unmodified pig.
Hyperacute rejection doesn't happen.
The minutes-to-hours catastrophic rejection is gone.
The organ pinks up after the clamps come off and stays pink.
The animal can urinate, or the heart can beat, for days or weeks.
Now, here's where I have to be careful.
The GalT knockout solved hyperacute rejection.
It did not solve rejection.
What it did was kick the can down the road.
Once hyperacute is out of the picture, you get a slower process — called acute humoral xenograft rejection, sometimes abbreviated AHXR — that kicks in over days.

It looks different at the microscope, but functionally it's the same idea: the human's immune system, now given a little more time, still recognizes the pig organ as foreign through other antigens, and still mounts a destructive response.
Why?
Because alpha-gal is the loudest signal, but it isn't the only one.
Pigs have other sugars on their cells, also absent in humans, also recognized by human antibodies.
The two big ones are a sugar called Neu5Gc — N-glycolylneuraminic acid — made by an enzyme called CMAH; and a glycan called Sda, made by an enzyme called β4GalNT2.
Humans lack both.
Both elicit antibodies.
Both contribute to graft attack.
So after the 2002 GalT knockout, the field had a clear roadmap.
The single knockout wasn't enough.
You needed to knock out alpha-gal, Neu5Gc, and Sda — all three — to strip the major xenoantigens off the pig surface.
That goal was called the triple knockout, or TKO.
And in the era of homologous recombination, achieving a TKO was nearly impossible.
Each knockout took years.
Stacking three of them — by breeding and re-cloning — was a project of monumental scale.
A few groups tried.
Some got two out of three.
Some got close.
What changed the game, around 2013, was the arrival of CRISPR.
The bacterial immune system, repurposed as a genome editing tool, made it possible to knock out multiple genes in the same fibroblast in a single experiment.
Suddenly, the triple knockout was easy.
Suddenly, you didn't have to stop at three.
You could pile on more edits.
You could knock out a dozen things at once.
You could insert human genes in addition to deleting pig genes.
The field's ambition expanded as soon as the tooling stopped being a bottleneck.
And once you piled on enough edits, the survival numbers in baboon preclinical experiments started to climb — from days, to weeks, to months, to, eventually, a record of over two years.
By the late 2010s, the field believed, for the first time, that a clinical attempt in humans was plausible.
That belief is what made David Bennett's surgery thinkable.
But before we get to Bennett, I have to tell you what's actually inside the modern xeno pig — gene by gene.
That's the next segment.
